summary: Gruber clarifies his earlier hypothesis that security problems are driving regular (non-technical) people to consider alternatives to Windows. He emphasizes this isn't about a mass exodus but a subtle shift of a few percentage points — something unprecedented for Windows, which has only ever gained market share. He argues most switchers will likely move to desktop Linux rather than Mac, but the mere fact that ordinary people are becoming aware alternatives exist is enormously beneficial to Apple. He concludes that rising Linux market share comes at Windows' expense, not the Mac's.
key_points: Windows has never before experienced a loss in market share momentum, making even a small decline historically significant
Most regular (non-technical) computer users don't even realize alternatives to Windows exist — security concerns are changing that awareness
The majority of Windows refugees will likely switch to desktop Linux rather than Mac, but increased awareness of alternatives benefits Apple regardless
Desktop Linux gaining market share comes at Windows' expense, not the Mac's, despite press framing it as bad news for Apple
Apple's biggest historical challenge isn't losing head-to-head comparisons but getting people to consider the Mac at all
money_quotes: Windows has never really suffered a loss in momentum. Its market share has only ever gone in one direction: up.
Most regular people don't even realize that there exist any choices other than Windows.
Nerds are people who at least realize Windows is a choice. I've never seen any sign that large numbers regular people are even aware of this. Until now.

The simple fact that more people are becoming aware that there are choices other than Windows, and that there are easy-to-understand reasons why one might do so, could be the best thing to happen to Apple's market share in a decade.
Here's a simple 'regular person' test: Can you explain the difference between RAM and a hard disk? The vast majority of computer users can't.
Rising desktop Linux market share comes at the expense of Windows' market share, not the Mac's.
And anything that raises awareness that there exist alternatives to Windows is a good thing for everyone other than Microsoft.
key_insight: The unprecedented fact that ordinary, non-technical people are becoming aware that alternatives to Windows exist — driven by security frustrations — represents a subtle but historically significant shift in momentum that benefits everyone except Microsoft.
